author: catwith1hat
content: That's very cool! My audit stuff above was somewhat agents free-styling the mapping. How do you keep that up to date?

author: stefanbratanov
content: Hey, it uses specrefs e.g. https://github.com/Consensys/teku/tree/master/specrefs which are based on ethspecify schemas https://github.com/ethereum/ethspecify they are static and maintained by clients so not up to date always, basically grab latest commit from each client for these files

author: stefanbratanov
content: Agents could be more accurate I presume for filling the gaps

author: jtraglia
content: Oh this is really neat! And yeah, not all clients have specrefs yet. I have been chatting with <@602753420033785856> from Lighthouse about it, but they're skeptical of their value. And [we made a branch](https://github.com/0xTylerHolmes/nimbus-eth2/tree/add-specrefs) with some specrefs for Nimbus (cc <@654267572107083777>) but never completed it & therefore never made the PR 😢

author: tersec
content: I don't understand this, the .yml file just seems to be a copy of the various Python spec functions?

author: jtraglia
content: It's auto-generated.

author: jtraglia
content: You bump the version, re-run, and it updates everything. It will also add new spec items to the files.

author: jtraglia
content: It's just a convinient way of keeping track of spec changes.

author: jtraglia
content: https://github.com/ethereum/ethspecify

author: tersec
content: interesting

author: jtraglia
content: You could also have these as inline comments in your codebase.

author: jtraglia
content: But most clients seem to prefer centralized files.

author: tersec
content: like, have the Python code as an inline comment?

author: jtraglia
content: Yup. I can find an example.

author: jtraglia
content: https://github.com/OffchainLabs/prysm/blob/9ec345e0fda9439740ac564eefb3a9173ead3cf5/beacon-chain/core/gloas/builder_exit.go#L10-L33

author: tersec
content: that's not easily enough parseable/distinguishable enough really

author: tersec
content: what is this `hash` from/of?

author: tersec
content: Nimbus already links systematically to the spec reference URL available checked for any given function, in a more human-readable way when feasible, i.e. 1.7.0-alpha.7 (sometimes it's not yet in a spec release and we have to use a commithash). I'm not sure this system would 100% replace this, because while some spec functions translate 1:1, some cannot, and the more important information is when we've checked the equivalence, not what the upstream function looks like (which is already accessible)

author: tersec
content: I used to use a script to automate updating to these: https://github.com/status-im/nimbus-eth2/blob/stable/scripts/find_unchanged_consensus_spec_files.sh

author: jtraglia
content: That's just `sha256(python code)`. It's there to detect any changes. In your codebase, you have just this (`<spec fn="apply_deposit" fork="electra" hash="c723ce7b" />`) and if the hash changes you know something change but would need to reference the spec to see what exactly.

author: tersec
content: ok, this seems like it could be a more precise version of that for some of those. But we also use those spec references for non-just-Python-code-equivalence, but as a general pointer into the specs for English prose

author: tersec
content: the issue with that script is that a lot of AI-driven refactoring (+ other random cosmetic line-ending changes, etc lots of just pointless noise) ended up being used across all the files

author: tersec
content: with enough regularity it got tedious

author: tersec
content: need AI to ingest AI I guess

author: jtraglia
content: No AI with ethspecify. It's deterministic.

author: jtraglia
content:
You might like some of the others styles btw. Eg:
* https://github.com/ethereum/ethspecify#link
* https://github.com/ethereum/ethspecify#diff

author: jtraglia
content: If you're interested you should look into using it in your codebase.

author: jtraglia
content: I can help if you want, but probably best to just let you guys handle it.

author: tersec
content: yeah, we use something similar to the link style now

author: tersec
content: it could be a good way to make that more maintainable

author: tersec
content: and even if it only applied to some subset of them that would still be useful

author: tersec
content: I do have some concern about the longevity of these links

author: jtraglia
content: Yes me too. I was just thinking about that.

author: tersec
content: i.e. the GitHub URLs we use are designed to go down only in case of basically catastrophe

author: jtraglia
content: I'm wanting to transfer that website to a different domain in the future.

author: jtraglia
content: To something more stable. Like `consensus.ethereum.foundation/specs`

author: tersec
content: we specifically link certain tags/commits for example, not trunk branches

author: jtraglia
content: So I actually wouldn't suggest using the link style right now. Unless you're okay with a big change later.

author: tersec
content: and the GitHub repo could disappear but that would be unlikely, usually they're just abandoned

author: tersec
content: yeah, so I like the concept

author: tersec
content: but would want the metadata aspect more parseable (not inline) and set up not only for internal spec-maintenance per se, but also as a kind of documentation (as it is now) for why-do-we-even-have-this-code

author: tersec
content: i.e. to show that it maps to a spec thing in a _summarized_, human-readable way, not only for people/machines checking for equivalence

author: tersec
content: because we maintain/manage functions different if they're spec (we try to keep it 1:1 matching if feasible) vs non-spec (up to us)

author: jtraglia
content: If you explore using this & notice something you'd like to change, ping me and I can probably accommodate.

author: tersec
content: alright, sure

author: tersec
content: actually, I guess, more parseable != not inline. they're different things
